data.title: Marvell: A Rare AI Entry Point Before ASIC Demand Hits Full Volume
data.description:
For any company, the deepest value is typically created when the focus is narrowed down to highest growth arenas.

In my view, this seems to sum up the case for Marvell Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL). Marvell is a $53.7 billion data infrastructure semiconductor play, that has been deliberately reorienting itself into a pure-play enabler of AI data centers. Following its $2.5 billion divesture of its automotive ethernet business, the company has been clear on its strategic intention of reallocating company resources towards its highest growing verticals, namely AI-driven custom silicon and interconnect.

Moreover, Marvell is positioning itself in a very favorable broader tech dynamic where hyper-scalers are racing to develop custom chips, but at the same time, are seeking to reduce their reliance on external GPU suppliers such as Nvidia and AMD. The largest tech plays, such as Google, Meta, Amazon, and Microsoft have already been developing their own custom chips to optimize their respective AI workloads.

Most hyper-scalers out there, however, lack the design ecosystem and manufacturing scale to develop custom chips in-house, and continue to prioritize focusing on their platform offerings.

Marvell stands to fill this critical gap in the space, leveraging its tech leadership in custom chips, its deep IP portfolio, and its strong track record in this domain. This is a massive opportunity for the company, and it stands to establish itself as a critical enabler in the custom chip space. Unlike in the commodity silicon chips market, where competition can be fierce, custom chip design wins lead to sticky, multi-year engagements that extend across successive product generations.

To add to this, the value opportunity in MRVL stock seems very compelling, and sets it up as an asymmetric play, where today’s seemingly high price tag could quickly normalize as earnings scale. With Marvell, investors have an opportunity to capture long-term compounding in one of the fastest-growing areas in tech.

I am initiating coverage on MRVL with a Buy rating.

Sharpening Focus on AI Data Centers

Broadly speaking, we see a clear narrative from the management of the company, that Marvell is deliberately narrowing its focus to stand as a pure-play AI data center enabler. This, of course, was pretty much solidified, following its $2.5 billion divesture of its automotive ethernet business. The proceeds of this cash deal have been deployed to two important areas: Firstly, towards an extended shareholder buyback program, in order to boost shareholder returns. Secondly, and far more importantly, we see clear signals that the management intends for these proceeds to provide the dry powder for reinvestment into the company’s core AI technology platforms.

This strategic reallocation of both capital and focus towards AI data center silicon and interconnect highlights the management's deliberate plans to double down on the company's highest growth vertical, while also tightening its alignment with hyper-scaler priorities. This clearly opens up a path to a much cleaner business model that prioritizes value creation for shareholders, as each incremental dollar of investment would ultimately feed back into expanding market share in one of the highest growth industries in the world right now.

For Marvell, its data center business, supported by AI tailwinds, saw a 73% YoY rise in revenue from $1.7 billion in the first six months of 2024 to $2.9 billion in the first six months of 2025. This is impressive, considering that overall topline excluding data centers, during this same time frame, had only grown by 31.7%. Clearly, just looking at these numbers, it would make greater sense, in terms of capital efficiency, to focus on the 73% growth vertical over the combined 32% verticals.

Enabling Hyperscaler Sovereignty

In light of Marvell's re-orientation towards capturing outsized data center growth, it would be important to highlight the company's most promising lever towards this end, which is its custom silicon strategy. Unlike in the case of commodity silicon, where margins are typically compressed, and there is minimal room to implement differentiation from competitors, custom design wins lead to far more long lasting partnerships with hyper-scalers that extend into multiple product generations.

This is exactly where Marvell's leadership in application specific integrated chips [ASICs] becomes so critical to consider. Marvell is able to co-design ASICs that are uniquely tailored to the performance and efficiency needs of each hyper-scaler. It achieves this by leveraging its deep IP portfolio across compute, networking, storage, and security. As a result, the company collaborates closely with cloud providers to roll out successive chip generations, locking in both revenue visibility and customer stickiness.

In fact, strides on this front are likely to position Marvell to stand out as the custom silicon arms dealer behind hyper-scaler in-house chips. Let me explain the dynamic at play for this to make better sense.

Giant hyper-scalers such as Google, Meta, Amazon, and Microsoft are increasingly choosing to design their own custom chips in order to optimize their workloads, and also to reduce their reliance on third party tech (such as that belonging to Nvidia or AMD, for example). These in-house designs deliver benefits in the form of power efficiency, latency, and workload-specific optimization, and allow these players to achieve strong performance in AI training, inference, networking, and security.

Most hyper-scalers, however, lack both the internal manufacturing scale and design ecosystem to take things smoothly from the architecture to silicon stages at scale. This is why a top ASIC player such as Marvell stands to benefit disproportionately by filling this crucial gap. In its recent presentation at the Citbank 2025 Global Technology, Media And Telecommunications Conference, the company's CEO, Matthew Murphy, highlighted that their company projects the TAM in the custom silicon sphere alone to expand to $55 billion by 2030, of which Marvell aims to capture at least 20% of the market. This is in addition to the $94 billion data center TAM projected by 2028, as discussed in the company's latest earnings call.

Importantly, Murphy highlighted that this is not simply an optimistic aspiration, but more of a precisely estimated outcome based on visible design wins already in the funnel. For Marvell, this is clearly a gamechanger, as the company's design pipeline has never been this deep, given its multiple high profile engagements at this stage that can each drive $500m+ in revenue run-rate.

This overall dynamic has me quite excited about Marvell's future, and in particular about the position it is building amid the wider AI ecosystem. We see clear evidence that the company is seeking to become a crucial enabler of hyper-scaler sovereignty, and thus, reducing dependency on players like Nvidia or AMD.

Valuation Assessment

MRVL is currently trading at a price of $62.31 (at the time of writing this article). This gives it a current market capitalization of ~$53.7 billion. Its trailing twelve month [TTM] revenue of ~$7.2 billion, therefore implies a TTM price-to-sales ratio of 7.5. This is more than double the tech sector median PS ratio of ~3.3x. Of course, this does not make MRVL stock appear cheap, at a surface level, at least.

We have to keep in mind, however, that in its recent Q2 results, the company has seen its topline grow by an impressive 58% YoY, with its data center business delivering 73% growth, as discussed above. On a TTM basis, this topline growth amounted to 37%, which still surpasses the tech sector median growth rate of 6.7%, by a substantial lead.

Moreover, even though the company is in a negative earnings state, it's forward PE [GAAP] ratio stands at 28, compared to the tech sector median of 30. To make sense of this, we need to keep in mind that the company appears to be pushing towards a breakeven and beyond at a rapid pace:

When breakeven is inevitably achieved (in the next few quarters as I expect), PE ratios may seem quite inflated, but that shouldn't spooks shareholders, because the upward climb in earnings is likely to be sharp. Considering that that the heavy lifting in custom silicon and AI infrastructure design wins is already leading to major progress, we can safely conclude that the revenue pipeline is shifting from early-stage ramps into multi-year production cycles.

As a result, I expect gross margins to expand quite meaningfully as volume continues its upward scaling trajectory. As it is, Marvell's TTM gross margin of 49.6% is pretty much in line with the tech sector median gross margin of 49.5%, at this early stage. Despite this, the company boasts a TTM EBITDA margin of 29.3% compared to the broader sector's median of 10.7%.

Marvell pretty much proves it can deliver strong operating leverage even before the full scale of AI-driven volume kicks in. Once hyper-scaler demand accelerates through production ramps, incremental revenues should fall disproportionately to the bottom line, as the company already has a strong gross margin starting point at this early stage.

Given this broader picture, I believe current prices pose an asymmetric opportunity for an entry into the stock. As such, I am initiating my coverage on MRVL stock with a buy rating.

Risk to Thesis

While I believe the hyper-scaler sovereignty angle is the most compelling aspect to the Marvell narrative, it also, sort of, introduces strategic uncertainty to this overall thesis. On the one hand, Marvell is establishing itself as a bridge for hyper-scalers that aim to achieve silicon sovereignty, but lack the technical capabilities to actually develop custom-chips in-house. On the other hand, however, the very fact that hyper-scalers are seeking to eliminate their reliance on external players could potentially imply that Marvell's role could be transitional for many of its clients. In fact, the largest hyper-scalers out there are already making strides in developing their own custom chips to optimize their workloads. If this trend were to continue, Marvell might see some of its largest contracts eventually discontinue, and ultimately see its relevance diminish.

That being said, however, even if the insourcing trend were to accelerate significantly in the future, I believe that there will always be a segment of the market made up of players that choose to focus their capital and talent on higher-level software, platforms, and customer-facing services, as opposed to tying their capital up in the costly, high-risk, and complex process of developing custom chips.

In other words, I believe Marvell's offerings to always be strategically sound for a vast part of the market. However, I will continue to monitor announcements of new hyperscaler partnerships in the custom silicon front. Of course, any slowdown or reduction in visibility could be an early red flag.

Investor Takeaway

Marvell Technology presents a compelling and asymmetric opportunity to shareholders. With its sharpened focus on the AI opportunity, and in particular its strategic strides to capitalize on hyper-scaler's need for silicon sovereignty, the company is positioning itself at the center of one of the fastest-growing verticals in the market.

While its multiples may not exactly appear discounted, relative to its peers in the tech space, the value proposition is highly attractive, once we consider the depth and visibility of Marvell’s growth trajectory.

I rate the stock as a buy.

data.title: NIO Can Benefit From Shifting Market Dynamics
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle marker Nio Inc. (NYSE:NIO) has seen its stock gain more than 40% this year. I believe there is still strong upside ahead despite some near-term pressures.

An introduction to Nio Inc.

Nio was founded in 2014 and is headquartered in Shanghai. The company filed for an initial public offering in 2018 and currently trades on the New York Stock Exchange. Nio is focused on electric vehicle technology and operates two Chinese manufacturing plants, alongside JAC Group.

Earnings release highlights expected mix of hope and fear

Nio’s latest earnings delivered a mix of optimism and strategic growth expectations, tempered by some near-term issues.

One of the key takeaways from the second quarter was a growing trend in deliveries. The company delivered 72,056 electric vehicles, marking a 25.6% increase year-over-year. That momentum continued in July and August, with 21,017 and 31,305 deliveries respectively, which should contribute to a strong full year figure.

Management has said that they expect total deliveries to range between 87,000 and 91,000 vehicles, representing a significant year-over-year growth of 40.7% to 47.1%.

That figure is still low in comparison to the likes of Tesla (TSLA) with a 384k delivery figure for Q2 2025. However, that has slumped from 495k in Q4 of 2024, so the companies are trending in the opposite direction.

Tesla’s deliveries are only 10,394 in vehicles outside of the flagship Model Y, while Nio has a more diverse portfolio. Nio’s August deliveries were comprised of 10,525 vehicles from the premium EV brand NIO, 16,434 vehicles from the family-oriented brand ONVO, and 4,346 vehicles from the smaller high-end electric car brand FIREFLY.

A benefit for the company going forward is that they have fresh vehicles coming online that could add increase market share. The family-focused and spacious ONVO L90 SUV was officially launched on July 31. Meanwhile, the flagship ES8 premium SUV is now being made available for orders delivered in late-September.

EV trends and the Chinese consumer

One of the key reasons I see strong upside in the company would be on Chinese consumer trends. It was well-known that China’s post-lockdown financial performance stuttered, and consumers were seen shunning big ticket items and tightening their belts due to the ongoing property market stress.

A budget conscious consumer has been a key tailwind for success at Nio’s Chinese EV rival BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF). China's largest automaker told analysts in March it was targeting sales of 5.5 million vehicles for 2025, but that figure has now been downgraded to 4.6 million, with analysts fearing that the company’s strong growth phase was cooling.

However, a reason for this slowdown has been the Chinese government’s crackdown on industry price wars back in August. BYD was growing in a perfect storm of budget-conscious consumers and the ability to squeeze rival manufacturers.

With that trend coming to a close, it levels the playing field for premium models like Nio. That can also flip to Nio’s favor when the Chinese consumer recovers to previous levels of confidence.

Analysts at Jeffries said BYD’s “historic playbook” of leveraging its supply chain for price cuts was ending.

"In short, BYD's 'gravy train' — fueled by scale, cost cuts, and tech leadership — has lost speed. Until it regains momentum, underperformance looks likely."

Ahead of the government shakeup, China’s EV sales still posted growth of around 35% year-on-year. The country’s sales were on track to register 20 million sales units in 2025.

Demand will continue for EVs and I believe the crackdown on price cuts should benefit Nio.

Valuation and ownership themes have upside

On the valuation front, Nio and BYD both trade at similar levels, with a price/sales ratio of 1.46x on a trailing twelve month basis, compared to 1.06x respectively. It should be noted that Tesla has a price/sales TTM of 11.21x, despite a recent sales slowdown.

There are reasons for Tesla’s premium, due to its superior data and a desire to leverage robotics and full self-driving technology. However, I believe that foreign appetite for Chinese shares has been a drag over the previous years.

Nio still has a big gap in institutional ownership footprint compared to Tesla, at 8.93% and almost 50% respectively. I believe Nio can close some of that gap as it matures and moves toward a more stable profitability.

Profitability trends look set to improve

As Nio seeks to become profitable, the new EV models are being released at the same time as management have been pursuing greater efficiency.

“Starting from the second quarter, our comprehensive cost reduction and efficiency improvement initiatives have started to yield results. Excluding organizational optimization charges, our non-GAAP operating loss improved by over 30% sequentially,” said CFO Stanley Yu Qu.

“We are now approaching a structural inflection point in our financials, with positive momentum building toward a sustainable virtuous cycle and continued performance improvements.,” he added.

Loss from operations in Q2 came in at US$685.2 million, for a decrease of 5.8% from the same period a year ago. It was also a 23.5% drop from the first quarter of 2025. The company posted a net loss of US$697.2 million, which was 26.0% lower than the first quarter of 2025. Cash and equivalents at the end of the second quarter was US$3.8 billion.

Risks to the investment thesis

The risk to the Buy recommendation is the current industry dynamics as Nio will have to continue to post stronger deliveries in order to limit losses.

“The listed EV makers have to cut their losses [because] it is difficult to raise fresh funds from investors” amid persistent concerns about overcapacity, said Ding Haifeng, a consultant at Integrity.

"Heavy losses over the coming quarters would threaten some Chinese EV builders, making them likely to be expelled from the highly competitive market.”

Nio’s CEO William Li has committed to delivering 50,000 EVs per month in the fourth quarter, which is a lofty target and could affect the share price if the company falls short.

Conclusion

Nio shares have gained more than 40% this year and I believe that this is simply the foundation being set for a long-term move higher. Vehicle deliveries are up more than 40% year-over-year, but changing dynamics in the domestic sector could be more favorable for premium brands like Nio in the coming months and years. There are some near-term pressures that need to be considered and I would adjust the position size accordingly to factor in those risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: lululemon: A 56% YTD Drop And Still No Catalysts In Sight
data.description:
lululemon athletica's (NASDAQ:LULU) (NEOE:LULU:CA) Q2-2025 earnings report wasn't great and confirmed what many investors already expected: weak sales in the US, product fatigue, and tariff-related pressures. Currently, the stock is down 18.6% following the report and is down 56% year-to-date. Nonetheless, Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts are generally bullish on the stock.

Two post-earnings SA analysis articles have been posted, with one from author Mehul Singh being a "Strong Buy" and another one from Gary Alexander being a "Buy." For example, Gary Alexander is optimistic due to accelerating international growth, high gross margins, a large cash position (which can be used for buybacks or growth initiatives), and a low valuation.

That's a reasonable take, but I still don't think there's enough to warrant immediate bullishness. The stock is undoubtedly cheap based on traditional metrics, but there's no predictable or clear short-term catalyst, and there could be more pain ahead in the North American part of the business. Additionally, downward revisions have become a common theme for LULU, which is never a good sign. Therefore, this seems like a "show-me" story.

A "Hold" rating seems more appropriate for now, although I'm not ruling out that the company can recover in the long term. It's just that, as of now, betting on when a recovery will happen is more speculative.

What Went Wrong For lululemon athletica In Q2

lululemon's Q2 revenue grew by 6.8% year-over-year (6% on a constant-currency basis) to $2.53 billion, missing expectations by $40 million. While overall comparable sales grew by 1%, Americas comps fell 3% on a constant-currency basis, following a 1% decline in Q1. That's a red flag for investors despite international comparable sales growing by 13% in constant currency.

Traffic worsened throughout the quarter as well, with May being the strongest month and July being the weakest. Further, management lowered full-year revenue growth expectations to 2-4% (or 4-6% if you exclude 53rd week from Q4 2024), which was lower than the previous forecast of 7-8% growth when excluding the 53rd week.

Combined with an EPS guidance cut (from a previous midpoint of $14.68 to a new range of $12.77–12.97), things don't look great, despite Q2 GAAP EPS of $3.10 (down 1.6% YoY) actually beating expectations by $0.25.

What Caused lululemon's Issues?

Product fatigue is a core problem, particularly in the lounge and social categories, which make up 40% of the business. Management acknowledged this. Here's what CEO Calvin McDonald said during the Q2 earnings call:

Our lounge and social product offerings have become stale and have not been resonating with guests. Specifically, we have seen a less enthusiastic response to some core franchises across lounge and social, such as Scuba, Softstreme and Dance Studio. And we feel that our opportunity remains in frequency and conversion, which is impacting their total spend. The data related to engagement and loyalty of our guests remains strong. My view now is that we have relied on the same product playbook across certain categories for too long.

Meanwhile, new items like Align No Line, Daydrift, and BeCalm are seeing better results, and the company plans to boost new product mix from 23% to 35% by next spring. I view this as a positive. However, that's next spring. These changes won't start showing up in LULU's financials for another year.

Competition is another hurdle, of course. During the Q2 earnings call, CEO Calvin McDonald stated, "The competitive landscape is different today than it was even 2 or 3 years ago. And while no single competitor is having a meaningful impact on our business, there are now many players in the market."

In other words, no single competitor is materially affecting the business, but the competition as a whole is clearly affecting lululemon. It's hard for companies to differentiate themselves in this industry.

At the same time, macro challenges persist. Plus, higher markdowns and tariffs also hurt profitability. For example, LULU's gross margin declined by 110 basis points to 58.5%, although that's still a strong gross margin.

Of the 220 basis points of tariff-related margin pressure expected this year, 170 basis points will come from the elimination of the de minimis exemption.

The de minimis exemption allowed products coming into the US valued under $800 to enter duty-free, meaning companies like lululemon could ship small e-commerce orders from Canada to the US without paying tariffs.

International Growth Is Good But Might Not Be Enough

The bright spot in Q2 was the international performance. International revenue grew 20% year-over-year on a constant-currency basis, reaching roughly $767 million, as you can see below, with $392.9 million from Mainland China and $374.1 million from Rest of World.

China comps rose 16%, and Rest of World comps grew 9% (constant currency), continuing the trend of revenue diversification outside of the Americas for lululemon. International revenues represented 30.4% of total Q2 revenues. In Q2 2024, International revenue of $629.645 million made up 26.6% of total sales.

Some may see this as a reason to buy, but it wasn't enough of a reason to buy the stock in the past year and likely still isn't now. Quite frankly, US-based investors care more about North American growth. They just generally treat it with more importance and "trust" it more, at least from what I've seen. lululemon needs to return to comparable growth in the Americas in order to bounce back. We don't know when that will happen yet, even if we're talking low-single-digit growth.

LULU Stock - Cheap Valuation With No Clear Catalyst

With LULU stock now at ~$168, the company trades at 13.1x management's 2025 EPS estimate (at the midpoint). That's obviously low compared to its historical multiple, especially for a premium brand with over $1.1 billion in cash, no debt, and gross profit margins in the high 50s. LULU's five-year average forward P/E ratio is closer to 38x.

It's tempting to say that the low valuation alone makes it worth buying. But the low valuation still needs a catalyst in order to send the stock higher. Right now, it seems that Lululemon lacks one.

New product launches like Big Cozy and Loungeful are still on the way (expected in the back half of this year), and even when they arrive, they may need time to prove themselves.

While it's true that lululemon can pull an American Eagle (AEO) and use Sydney Sweeney or another viral celebrity for an ad, there's no guarantee that lululemon will do that or if it will even be executed properly. It's speculation. Speculating like that can be worth it when the valuation is low, so I see the appeal, but it's not something I'd invest a whole lot into, if investing at all.

I'd rather wait until LULU actually executes on a viral marketing campaign or delivers a "hit" product. With American Eagle stock, you had plenty of time after the Sydney Sweeney campaign to buy the stock and capture more gains.

lululemon Isn't A Broken Company

lululemon isn't a broken company. It still has a relatively high gross margin, lots of cash, and a loyal customer base, according to management.

Plus, management isn't in denial and is trying to turn things around. The recent hire of a Chief AI & Technology Officer, Ranju Das, shows that they're really trying.

Still, turnarounds take time. Right now, we're in a transition phase.

With the recent gap down, a short-term bounce wouldn't surprise me since the stock is quite oversold, but I don't expect a sustained rebound to the mid-200s unless it can solve its current issues or at least show signs of progress.

The Takeaway

lululemon's Q2 report highlighted the result of weakening demand in the Americas, product fatigue, and tariff pressures.

Meanwhile, International sales growth is encouraging, but for now, I don't think it's enough to offset the deterioration in the Americas.

The stock is definitely cheap compared to its historical valuation, as it trades at 13.1x forward earnings. That, combined with an oversold stock, stops me from giving it a Sell rating. However, with no clear catalyst to send shares higher, there's a risk of LULU stock being dead money for a bit before maybe rebounding in the longer term.

Lululemon still has a strong brand, enviable margins, and a cash-rich balance sheet, which should support a longer-term recovery, but near-term visibility is limited. LULU looks like a classic "show-me" story. Thus, I rate it as a Hold.

data.title: Broadcom: Multiple Expansion Still On The Table After Today's Pop
data.description:
In my last coverage of Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) (NEOE:AVGO:CA), I was ultra bullish on the margin expansion of the company. Back then, I was quite encouraged by the guidance for Q3:

The guidance for the next quarter was quite good: Q3 AI semi revenue expected at $5.1 billion, up 60% yoy, and infrastructure revenue projected at $6.7 billion, up 16%

Well, the company beat those figures, posting a 63% yoy growth in AI semiconductor revenue and 17% for the infrastructure segment. No doubt this was a record quarter, with a record backlog of $110 billion, which sharpens the visibility for the top line in the near term. Furthermore, software gross margin reached 93% in Q3 and operating margin ticked up to 77% after integrating VMware.

Overall, I remain bullish on the stock, even though the valuation may look a bit stretched when compared to some of its peers. The main drag that I see for the company is the non-AI semiconductor product line, which is dragging down the consolidated (AI + non-AI) semiconductor segment.

A Record-Breaking Quarter

From a fundamentals perspective, the company has been reporting record results, with a clear visibility into future revenue growth driven by its record backlog. Let's start with the basics. In Q3, revenue was $16.0 billion, up 22% yoy, and adjusted EBITDA came in at $10.7 billion, up 30% yoy.

A quick side note. The increase in revenue growth was organic, meaning that the effect of the VMware acquisition in 2023 (that's why the quarters ending in February 2024 through February 2025 have seen a spike in revenue growth) had nothing to do with it.

That is not to say VMware didn't contribute to the recent growth. In fact, the infrastructure segment has seen its revenue go up by 17% yoy in the last quarter ($6.8B vs management's previous guidance of $6.7B), with the company releasing VMware Cloud Foundation version 9.0. This is a fully integrated cloud platform that "enables enterprises to run any application workload, including AI workloads, on virtual machines and on modern containers", according to CEO Hock Tan. Why did I mention that?

Well, it seems that over 90% of the company's top 10k accounts from vSphere have bought the VMware Cloud Foundation platform, so any improvements to this product line are music to my ears. On the guidance side, FQ4 software revenue is projected at $6.7B, representing an increase of 15% yoy.

Moving on to the semiconductor segment, revenue from the AI semiconductors was $5.2 billion in Q3, up 63% yoy. By the way, this was the 10th consecutive quarter of robust growth for this segment, driven by Tomahawk 5 and the Jericho3 Ethernet fabric router. By the way, the company recently released Jericho4 and the Tomahawk 6, which I believe are nice catalysts for further growth. Speaking of semiconductors, total revenue came in at $9.2 billion, up 26% yoy. In Q4, the company projects $6.2 billion in AI revenue, representing a 66% yoy increase from the same period last year. Furthermore, management was quite optimistic about the growth for FY 2026, citing four XPU customers (up from 3 in the previous quarter):

Last quarter, one of these prospects released production orders to Broadcom, and we have accordingly characterized them as a qualified customer for XPUs and, in fact, have secured over $10 billion of orders of AI racks based on our XPUs. And reflecting this, we now expect the outlook for our fiscal 2026 AI revenue to improve significantly from what we had indicated last quarter.

Unfortunately, the recovery in non-AI semis is slow and uneven, which is dragging down the growth rate in this segment. More on this topic in the risk section.

In terms of future revenue growth, the company is in a sweet spot. The Street is projecting double-digit growth, around ~20% yoy for each quarter until the end of 2026.

This decent growth is supported by strong bookings, with a consolidated backlog reaching a record $110 billion. On the company's side, management is projecting Q4 revenue at $17.4B, representing a 24% yoy increase. If achieved, that would be a 200 bps increase from the last quarter. Furthermore, EBITDA margin is projected at 67% of total revenue (the same value as in the last quarter).

Valuation And Price Action

I admit it not not easy to find a direct peer to do a valuation analysis. Looking at the semiconductor business, in my view, Marvell Technology could be the closest like-for-like peer on cloud/AI networking and custom ASICs. There is also some overlap with Nvidia on data center networking (Spectrum Ethernet/InfiniBand). On the infrastructure segment, I see Nutanix as the closest public comp to VMware. On mainframe and platform software, I believe IBM could be a good peer, and in infrastructure and database, I see Oracle as a mature benchmark. That said, here is the comparison analysis between these players:

As noted above, Broadcom is trading at premium multiples when compared to its peers. On a TTM P/E basis, the company is trading at 111x last year's earnings. That's a rich premium, not just when compared to its peers, but also when compared to its historical range.

So, do these high multiples put me off?

Not really. I think the narrative of the stock is very strong in the midst of the current AI supercycle, and I believe the future growth that the Street is projecting until the end of the decade justifies a further multiple expansion.

That said, there are some risks to consider.

Risks

In Q3, the non-AI semi revenue came in at $4.0 billion, flat compared to Q2. This is dragging down the growth in the consolidated semiconductor segment (i.e., AI and non-AI semiconductor products).

In terms of end markets, broadband was up, enterprise networking and server-storage were down, and wireless and industrial were flat in Q3. The guidance for Q4 in this segment is $4.6 billion, in the low double digits on a sequential basis. The overall recovery for this segment is U-shaped. The CEO said that a meaningful recovery won't be realized until the middle and late part of FY26.

By the way, this is one of the reasons why I believe a further multiple expansion is feasible. Once the non-AI product line recovers next year, I believe we could see a further improvement in the top line of the semiconductor segment from 2027 onwards. In the meantime, this remains an overhang for the semis segment.

Another risk is related to the recent price action.

Considering the post-earnings spike, there could be a small pullback in the next days. Over a 12-month timeframe, I believe such a pullback will be just noise.

Looking Ahead

In the near term, I see Broadcom's setup as straightforward: AI keeps compounding, the semiconductor backlog derisks some of the top-line growth in the next quarters, and the margin expansion looks quite promising. Starting with the latter, infrastructure software gross margin was 93% in Q3 (vs 90% a year ago), and after the integration of VMware, operating margin ticked up to 77% compared to 67% in Q3 FY24. I expect margins to remain in this range, supported by

In the semiconductor segment, the AI product line growth remains intact. Management now projects $6.2 billion in AI revenue for the current quarter, up 66% yoy. Furthermore, the company posted a record consolidated backlog, sitting at $110 billion.

On AI momentum, I see the hyperscalers' CapEx plans intact (see here and here), and the adoption of the newly released Jericho4 and TomaHawk 6 is a nice catalyst ahead. Overall, I reiterate my strong buy rating on Broadcom.

data.title: Aquestive Therapeutics: Expect Its Torrid Pace To Relax (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This is my second Aquestive Therapeutics (NASDAQ:AQST) article after 03/2025's "Aquestive Therapeutics: Oral Anaphylaxis NDA Opportunity Spells Buy". In Spells Buy I rated Aquestive as a "Buy". Over the ensuing months it has cooperated nicely increasing by a tasty ~50%.

In this article I will evaluate its potential for further increases; I will reference its 08/12/2025 Q2/2025 earnings Call, 10-Q, and Presentation.

On its way to its 01/31/2026 PDUFA, Aquestive got an advisory committee jolt.

As discussed in Spells Buy, Aquestive has a variety of small ball sublingual film products that generate insubstantial revenues. Its Anaphylm epinephrine sublingual film, promises to materially change this narrative. The FDA recently accepted Aquestive's Anaphylm NDA, setting an 01/31/2026 PDUFA date.

In doing so it reiterated that it may decide to conduct an Advisory Committee meeting for the approval of Anaphylm. During the Call, CEO Barber advised:

...We recently submitted our 120-day safety update to the FDA. I'm pleased to say that there was nothing new or of consequence to report in this safety update. We also believe the FDA is about to conclude its mid-cycle review. While we do not participate in this review, we would expect to have more clarity on whether we will have an advisory committee meeting once the FDA has fully processed its mid-cycle review meeting.

Within short order on 09/04/025, CEO Barber was able to announce the FDA decision that an advisory committee meeting was not required for approval of Anaphylm. This decision sent the stock into a hyper-growth spurt as shown below:

On 09/03/2025 Aquestive closed at $3.95. It opened on 09/04/2025 at $4.29; from there it roared upwards to a high of $5.10 on heavy volume of >13 million shares. On 09/05/2025 it continued its race upwards; it hit a high of $5.48. As I write on midafternoon of 09/05/2025 it is settling back at $5.28. It closed the day close to its high at $5.39.

The market excitement seems well merited. Aquestive's Anaphylm is an immensely attractive product. The Presentation makes a compelling case. Slide 12, "Anaphylaxis is on the Rise—and so are the Risks", describes the need:

  1. ~40 million patients with type 1 allergic reactions;
  2. ~20 million patients diagnosed and under physician care from 2020 to 2023;
  3. ~5 million scripts for epinephrine were filled in 2024;
  4. ~100 deaths per year in the U.S. due to anaphylaxis.

Slide 13 estimates that the epinephrine market is growing from $800 million in 2024 to $2 billion by 2031, with a CAGR of 15% from 2021 to 2023. The key differentiator which distinguishes Anaphylm from competitive epinephrine products is its sublingual mode of delivery.

The product itself is the size of a stick of gum as shown by Presentation slide 10 below:

Contrast this with ARS Pharmaceuticals' newly approved neffy which I recently discussed in 07/2025's "ARS Pharmaceuticals: High Risk High Reward Rollout Of No Needle Epinephrine". The difference is stark. Ars' stock has gone down ~42% since the article was published. During this same period Aquestive stock has risen by more than 50%.

The reasons are multiple. Anaphylm's sublingual film is far more attractive than neffy's bulky spray delivery device. Not only is Anaphylm more convenient, importantly it is more effective as shown by Presentation slide 15 below:

Anaphylm's 12 minutes to reach peak absorption beats all its competitors, besting neffy by 18 minutes. There can be no question that Anaphylm has captured investors' imaginations. Will it be equally successful in capturing customers if it is approved? I am a believer that it will. We will have to stay tuned until after 02/2026 to see if I am correct.

With limited liquidity, Aquestive is working on developing a financial pathway to launch.

During the Call CFO Toth advised that cash and cash equivalents were $60.5 million as of 06/30/2025. On 08/14/2025 Aquestive announced pricing of a common stock offering at price of $4.00 per share with gross proceeds expected to be $85 million.

Its cash burn for H1/2025 was ~$31.3 million up from ~$17.4 in H1/2024. This was primarily due to its increase in its net loss, primarily relating to prelaunch spending. Its annual cash burn for 2024 increased substantially over its cash burn for 2023 as shown by its latest 10-K cash flow table excerpt below, for the same reason, an increase in its net loss for the period:

Obviously Aquestive's future will require significantly more liquidity resources than it has in the past. During the Call CEO Barber addressed this issue as it impacted Anaphylm's launch. He stated his perspective on pathways to financing the launch:

...We currently have multiple proposals in process, including for EU only rights and alternate financing vehicles. We are working through the pathways available to the company and will select the best pathway when the time is right. However, as of today, we remain confident that launch financing is available to the company and that we will have secured financing prior to launch.

Its share offering with its $85 million net will have boosted Aquestive's liquidity to ~$145.5 million. This should get it through the rest of 2025 with ~$100 of liquidity remaining. Depending on the trajectory of its launch this should get it through the bulk of 2026. At that point it will have the benefit of whatever deals it can fashion by slicing and dicing Anaphylm's ROW rights.

While I am not an expert, the following quote from Aquestive's latest 10-K gives me comfort as to the patent protection for Anaphylm:

Our patent portfolio currently comprises at least 110 issued patents worldwide, of which at least 28 are U.S. patents, and more than 150 pending patent applications worldwide. These issued patents and pending patent applications provide both process of making and composition of matter protection for our PharmFilm technology and products and product candidates, including Suboxone, Libervant and Anaphylm and our PharmFilm formulations of diazepam, clobazam, riluzole and epinephrine. These patents and, if issued as patents, pending patent applications will likely expire between 2024 and 2044. The pending patent applications will provide composition of matter and process of making protection for our PharmFilm dosage formulations of diazepam and epinephrine and, if issued as patents, will likely expire by 2040 and 2044, respectively. The projected expiration dates exclude any patent term adjustment or patent term extension. [emphasis added]

In terms of shelf life, both neffy and anaphylm perform well.

Conclusion

I am a strong believer in the potential of anaphylm. Nonetheless I am trepidatiously lowering my previous "Buy" rating for Aquestive to "Hold". I do this for two reasons. My first and foremost reason is because of my concern that Aquestive has moved too far, so fast that it needs to pull back a bit and consolidate its gains.

In my own small holding I have sold half my position. I expect to hold the remainder rain or shine, and if it pulls back I hope to buy back in. Beyond the frothiness in its share price, my other concern relates to pricing of anaphylm. I am waiting for further information as to management's approach to this delicate issue before committing further resources to this bame.

data.title: AppLovin: S&P 500 Vindication - At Last!
data.description:
Investment Thesis

AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) has had to endure a tough year so far of skepticism and cynicism to prove the long-term durability of its digital advertising business.

On one hand a flurry of bearish reports through this year kept driving unwanted attention towards AppLovin. On the other hand, the company’s shares always seemed to miss out on being added to the S&P 500 index, despite being one of the most eligible index-worthy candidates as I noted in my previous coverage on AppLovin.

Despite these transitory headwinds, AppLovin’s business has delivered ~22% revenue growth ytd while GAAP operating margins have expanded 1116 bp over the same period leading to the company’s shares jumping 51% during this time frame.

However, after a long wait, AppLovin & its investors have had the last laugh when the S&P published their updated index inclusion updates that showed the company’s shares will (finally!!) be added to the most watched benchmark index of the world - the S&P 500 (SP500).

With this inclusion, I suspect many global funds that track the S&P 500 will rush to re-weight their portfolios to get exposure to one of the fastest growing ad tech names in the industry.

I am revising my PT for AppLovin higher and maintaining my Buy rating for the stock.

AppLovin’s Focusing One One Goal: Digital Ad Domination

AppLovin was first up on my radar in April last year when I studied the company’s partnership with Alphabet’s GCP (Google Cloud Platform) and noticed the scope for potential operational leverage.

Since then I have strongly advocated for investors to get exposure to AppLovin’s shares due to the strong focus and resilient execution the company has demonstrated in gaining a larger share of the global ad budget with every quarter that goes by. This company has never failed to disappoint my expectations and has been delivering profitable revenue growth like clockwork.

2022 was a pivotal year for AppLovin, where the company migrated its entire ad platform onto GCP, which included running AppLovin’s training workloads, recommendation models, etc., all on GCP. The significant performance improvements due to the migration have immensely aided Axon 2.0, AppLovin’s ad recommendation engine, which powers the company’s digital marketing solutions, including the highly popular AppDiscovery.

AppLovin’s digital marketing solutions have been hugely popular with marketers not only because of the performance improvements and higher ROAS (return on ad spend), but also because AppLovin continues to offer a wide variety of high-performing ad inventory, which includes the new e-commerce domain that the company is already piloting with digital advertisers.

For investors, this translates into a consistent demonstration of resilient execution resulting in the clockwork-like profitable revenue growth that I mentioned earlier. Here are some simple numbers to back it up. Between 2018 and 2022, AppLovin’s revenues grew at a CAGR of ~55.4% to $2.82B. Unfortunately, during those 4 years, AppLovin erased all its margins to report an operating loss of ~$19.5M, erasing all its margins from the 48% GAAP margins it had reported in 2018.

Now, since 2022, AppLovin’s revenues have grown at a CAGR of ~33% to $5.7B. But during these two and a half years, GAAP margins have exploded by over 5000 bp, allowing AppLovin to report GAAP operating margins of 51%. Note that these are all on a TTM basis. On a quarterly basis, the company’s GAAP operating margins have risen all the way to 76%, per its Q2 report.

In terms of EBITDA, I expect AppLovin to deliver at least 80% EBITDA margins this year, which is ~800-900 bp higher than 2024’s EBITDA margins of 72%. The magnitude of EBITDA margin expansion is smaller versus what the company had delivered last year because AppLovin will be significantly ramping up its digital advertising solutions in the e-commerce space during the upcoming holiday season. But investors should still see a sizeable dollar flow through of EBITDA due to the rapid double-digit growth that is still expected from AppLovin.

Note: AppLovin may have seen slower growth in EBITDA had it not divested its Apps business for $900M earlier this year.

The divestment unlocks more operational leverage as well, strengthening the balance sheet. Q2’s report showed cash+equivalents at $1.19B, up 61% since the start of the year, versus debt+obligations of $3.5B, down 5.4% since the start of this year.

The point for investors to take home from all of this is AppLovin will continue to deliver profitable revenue growth like clockwork. There is going to be noise that corners of the market will always create, but AppLovin’s management appears to be relentless in its focus and unwavering in its execution on becoming a dominating force in the ad tech market.

Hopefully, AppLovin’s inclusion in the S&P 500 that was published by S&P Dow Jones vindicates the company from all the pessimism this year that has been distracting investors from the tenacity of AppLovin’s business fundamentals. S&P’s index inclusion note suggests AppLovin may replace MarketAxess (MKTX), resulting in the ad tech company accounting for 0.01% of the weight of the S&P 500 index. Bears may again swoop in here to say that AppLovin’s inclusion results in a much smaller than anticipated index weightage. But my message to investors remains unchanged—AppLovin is a solid company that is firmly on the path of ad tech domination and will continue to deliver strong profitable revenue growth.

AppLovin Shares Continue To Offer Upside

At the moment, AppLovin is expected to deliver EBITDA margins of >80%, implying an 800-900 bp margin expansion this year. Earnings per share are expected to grow by 104% to $9.25 on revenues of ~$5.6B, growing 18% y/y. Such growth rates warrant a forward PE of ~55-56x, which implies 19% upside from Friday’s close or a PT of $585 per share.

I continue to believe that the company’s shares must be valued at least at 55x or more due to the scope of revenue growth-fueled margin expansion that the company keeps delivering consistently, and current levels, despite the after-market increase, offer upside, conservatively speaking.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

AppLovin remains a highly volatile name, and short sellers have not fully backed off from the stock. ~5% of the company’s shares are still shorted, which can keep the stock volatile in the future. Additionally, if AppLovin’s e-commerce entry does not pan out as planned, it can hit the company’s growth. That’s because AppLovin estimates e-commerce is growing rapidly and should account for ~10% of the company’s revenues this year, making it a meaningful end market for AppLovin’s digital ad business for FY25 and beyond. So far, there have been no reasons for me to be skeptical of AppLovin’s e-commerce business pilots yet.

Takeaway

AppLovin’s inclusion in the S&P 500 has finally arrived and should vindicate the company’s shares over all the bearishness it has endured this year. The company’s S&P 500 inclusion should boost the company’s profile among all the funds tracking the S&P 500 index.

AppLovin has been one of my favorite ad tech stocks so far that I have covered, and I continue to hold a long-term view of the company’s fundamentals.

I reiterate my Buy rating on AppLovin and have upgraded my PT.

data.title: Regenxbio: Remains A "Buy Rating" Despite BLA Review Delay Of RGX-121 For Hunter Syndrome
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Regenxbio (NASDAQ:RGNX) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Regenxbio :Wet AMD ABBV-RGX-314 Results Remain On Track For 2026". In this particular article, I mainly spoke about the company advancing the use of ABBV-RGX-314 to treat patients with wet age-related macular degeneration [Wet-AMD]. As far as this program goes it is entirely on track and the same timeline remains. For starters, Sura-vec, in terms of sub-retinal delivery in targeting patients with Wet-AMD is still on track in the two pivotal ATMOSPHERE and ASCENT clinical trials. That is, topline results from both of these late-stage studies are still expected in 2026. Plus, there is still the ability to advance this very same candidate via a different route of administration, which is with suprachoroidal delivery in the ongoing phase 2 AAVIATE study.

I briefly touched up on the opportunity that the company had in terms of its BLA submission to the FDA for the use of gene therapy RGX-121 for the treatment of patients with Hunter's syndrome [also known as MPS II]. However, this time around I want to go over updates specifically relating to this program. The FDA had set a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of November 9, 2025 originally. However, with the company needing to submit additional longer-term clinical data of the 13 patients being evaluated in the pivotal study, the U.S. agency needed additional time to review the application. With that being said, it had to extend the PDUFA date to a new one, which was set as February 8, 2026. This is a crucial review because RGX-121 could become the first to target the root cause of this disorder.

In terms of RGX-202 for the treatment of ambulatory Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD] patients in the phase 2/3 AFFINITY DUCHENNE pivotal study enrollment is going. Topline results from this pivotal study are expected in the 1st half of 2026, with the potential to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] of RGX-202 to the FDA for the targeting of these ambulatory DMD patients under the Accelerated Approval pathway in mid-2026. With all this being said, I believe that this stock should remain with a "Buy Rating" for the time being. I think that the potential to receive Accelerated Approval of RGX-121 for the treatment of patients with Hunter syndrome is huge. If the Accelerated Approval application is rejected, then I might keep this rating anyways. The reason why is because it will still have the collaboration agreement with AbbVie (ABBV) to develop Sura-vec for the treatment of patients with wet age-related macular degeneration [Wet-AMD] and diabetic retinopathy [DR].

RGX-121 Targeting Hunter Syndrome Patients Delayed But Not Cut

As I noted above, Regenxbio was wet to have its PDUFA target action review date of November 9, 2025. This is when the FDA was set to review and decide whether or not RGX-121 [Clemidsogene lanparvovec] should be approved to treat these patients with Hunter syndrome. However, there was a delay with this expected PDUFA date and I will be going over this and any other updates about this clinical program below. Before going over all of such updates, relating to the advancement of RGX-121, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. Mucopolysarcharidosis type II [MPS II], also known as Hunter Syndrome, is a type of neurodevelopmental disorder whereby the patient lacks the proper protein [enzyme] known as iduronate-2-sulfatase. This protein is necessary for people to be able to break down complex sugar molecules, known as glycosaminoglycans [GAGs]. The problem is that this protein doesn't work properly because of a mutation if the IDS gene, which is responsible creating the I2S enzyme. Without this ability to clear GAGs from the body it leads to a host of problems or symptoms that these patients experience. More specifically, I2S removes the sulfate chemical from a molecule known as alpha-L-iduronic acid. Why is this important? This molecule is found in two GAGs known as the following:

  • heparan sulfate
  • dermatan sulfate

The term heparan sulfate [HS] is going to be important because this is built up in the tissues of a patient and also in the central nervous system [CNS] as well. It is what ultimately causes the development delay found in these patients. In other words, it affects the brain and prevent the person from achieving developmental milestones. I do want to preface this to say, though, that physical parts of the body are also affected by Hunter syndrome. The Hunter syndrome market in the 7 major markets is expected to reach $1.18 billion by 2035. This is a very large market opportunity for sure and the company holds the potential to achieve a first.

That is, if RGX-121 is ultimately approved by the FDA, it would be the first one-time treatment of its kind to treat the root underlying cause of Hunter Syndrome. Why the need for a therapy such as this? Truth be told, there is an enzyme replacement therapy [ERT] , which is recombinant idursulfase ELAPRASE. This is ideal in terms of helping these patients a bit, but there still remains a lack of being able to help these patients in terms of the behavioral and neurocognitive development for them. The reason why is because this ERT does not cross the blood-brain barrier and therefore, does not affect the CNS to help in this regard.

In order to determine if RGX-121 was going to be effective in treating these patients with Hunter Syndrome, it ran a few studies. One such study was the phase 1/2/3 CAMPSIITE trial, which was set to recruit up to a total of 48 patients. Just today, September 5, 2025, the company release updated 12-month longer-term data from the pivotal portion of this trial, which had 13 patients. The latest data released showed that this gene therapy did well over this 12-month period, whereby patients achieved an average reduction of 82% of CSF levels of HS D2S6. This goes back to what I noted above, that high HS levels cause the developmental delay for these patients. Thus, it is believed that HS might act as a predictive surrogate biomarker that shows the translation to these patients seeing an improvement in cognitive ability. Furthermore, two other pieces of data were reported to prove this specific outcome. Statistical significance being achieved in CSF of HS D2S6 below maximum attenuated levels is one of such facts. Another data that happened was that patients had an 85% reduction of CSF HS D2S6 over a 2-year period. What remains a major issue though is that if the FDA ultimately agrees with this assessment that the 16-week data, along with longer-term 12 month data showing an increase in cognitive ability, is enough of a surrogate biomarker to predict such clinical benefit.

That is, whether or not this data is enough to allow the company to receive FDA approval under the Accelerated Approval pathway with the data submitted initially as part of its BLA of RGX-121 and then also with the newly obtained longer-term 12-month data I just went over above. Speaking of this newly release data today, this was submitted as part of information request from the FDA for the BLA package this gene therapy. With this newly submitted data, there is no way the FDA could have reviewed everything that quickly and thus why the PDUFA date was delayed. The original PDUFA target action date of RGX-121 for the treatment of patients with Hunter syndrome was extended to the new date of February 8, 2026.

Another positive development relating this very same program happened in August of 2025, whereby there was the successful completion of a pre-BLA inspection and other necessary inspections. The last thing I want to note about this program is that FDA Accelerated Approval is not only going to serve in adding a new form of treatment for these Hunter syndrome patients. Nor is it just going to help the company generate revenues, which is an enormous opportunity indeed. With U.S. marketing approval given, it is going to allow Regenxbio to receive a Priority Review Voucher [PRV]. Such a PRV can be sold for an enormous amount of cash, probably around $100 million or more. Just to give one example of this a company by the name of Bavarian Nordic was able to sell its PRV for a total of $160 million. It obtained this speedy review voucher because it received FDA approval of its chikungunya vaccine VIMKUNYA, prevention of disease caused by chikungunya virus in people 12 years of age and older.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Regenxbio had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $363.6 million as of June 30, 2025. The company had to burn through cash during the quarter, but this was offset with two particular transactions that happened during the most recent Q2 of 2025 earnings. One major financial boost came from a partnership it had made with Nippon Shinyaku. This deal was set that the company would be responsible for development of RGX-121 and RGX-111 in the United States and then Nippon would be responsible for development of these in licensed territories outside of the United States.

As part of this deal being made, Regenxbio received an upfront payment of $110 million. In addition, it could be eligible to receive up to $700 million in development and sales-based milestones as well. A second major financial inflow of cash came from a $250 million royalty bond agreement with Healthcare Royalty. This is a good deal, because this immediately brought in $150 million upfront. This was a good agreement done because with this, it was noted that it would extend its cash runway into early 2027.

This is only the beginning, because then the other tranches as part of this agreement are rapidly approaching. For starters, there is a $50 million payment set to come from this due to a ZOLGENSMA sales-based milestone being reached [potentially by April 30, 2027]. Secondly, another batch of $50 million is to be achieved thereafter with an agreement of both parties being established. This additional cash, should help the company fund its operations beyond the projected early 2027 date I noted directly above. The company burns roughly $79.4 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $59.5 million in R&D expenses and then $19.9 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Regenxbio. The first risk deals with the extended PDUFA target action date dealing with gene therapy RGX-121 for the treatment of patients with Hunter syndrome. The reason why is because the delay occurred because the FDA needed additional time to review the new 12 month longer-term data from the pivotal portion of the phase CAMPSIITE program. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this data response to the FDA is going to be good enough to warrant U.S. marketing approval of RGX-121 to treat these lysosomal storage disorder patients. Secondly, failure to receive FDA approval of it would also mean an inability for it to receive a PRV to sell for cash.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the collaboration that the company has with AbbVie, which is to develop Sura-vec for the treatment of patients with Wet-AMD and DR. In terms of the Wet-AMD development program, there are two ongoing pivotal trials for it, which are ATMOSPHERE and ASCENT. The point of these trials is to assess the use of subretinal delivery of Sura-vec for the treatment of these eye disorder patients. Topline results from these late-stage trials are expected in 2026. The risk here is that upon the release of data from both of these late-stage pivotal trials, there is no assurance that the primary efficacy endpoints are going to be met in a statistically significant manner. Lastly, the hope is to move from subretinal delivery to suprachoroidal delivery of this gene therapy targeting Wet-AMD patients in the phase 2 AAVIATE study. Even if the subretinal version of this therapy works, there is no guarantee that the company will be able to make the switch over to the route of administration of it via suprachoroidal delivery.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of another gene therapy candidate from its pipeline, which is RGX-202. This particular candidate is being evaluated in the pivotal cohort of the phase 2/3 AFFINITY DUCHENNE clinical study targeting patients with ambulatory DMD. Topline results from this study are expected to be released in the 1st half of 2026. If this particular pivotal study is successful, then the company believes it will be able to file a BLA of RGX-202 under an Accelerated Approval pathway. The risks here is that there is no assurance that the final results from this study will establish proof-of-concept of this gene therapy being able to help treat these patients with DMD. Secondly, even if the data is ideal enough for it to file a BLA under an Accelerated Approval pathway, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to approve it for U.S. marketing.

Conclusion

Regenxbio is still coming along well with respect to its pipeline since the last time I wrote an article about it. It is still pressing on with its late-stage trials of Sura-vec in collaboration with AbbVie to target patients with Wet-AMD. Both with this gene therapy being given via subretinal and suprachoroidal delivery. That is, the pivotal phase 3 studies for this program ATMOSPHERE and ASCENT remain on track. More importantly, there is a chance for this company to help give Hunter syndrome patients a new treatment option, one in which that could provide the ability to help them in terms of developmental delay.

With RGX-121 being able to cross the blood brain barrier, plus being delivered efficiently into the patient's CNS, it is believed that this could end up being a good disease-modifying therapy relating to helping improve cognitive functioning for them. The truth is that this RGX-121 program targeting patients with Hunter syndrome still remains at risk. The delay in the BLA filing was so that the FDA could have additional time to review the newly submitted 12-month data from the phase 1/2/3 CAMPSIITE study.

There is no way of knowing if this data is going to be sufficient enough for the FDA to grant U.S. marketing approval of RGX-121. There are two potential "firsts" possible here. That is, this gene therapy has the potential to be the first approved gene therapy and one-time form of treatment for Hunter syndrome patients. With the release of positive 12-month longer-term data from the phase 1/2/3 CAMPSIITE study, plus the upcoming PDUFA review date for this very same program set for February 8, 2026, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: Buffett Bets On Real Estate: New Holdings Revealed At Berkshire Hathaway
data.description:
Each quarter, big investment firms need to release 13F filings, which reveal what they have been buying and selling lately.

This gives an opportunity for smaller investors to review what legendary investors like Warren Buffett have been doing lately.

His holding company, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), just recently released this filing for Q2, and there is one main takeaway.

Warren Buffett appears to be betting on a real estate recovery.

Three out of their five largest investments in the last quarter were real estate plays:

1) Lennar Corp (LEN): Berkshire bought nearly a billion worth of Lennar, which is one of the biggest homebuilders in the United States. It owns a lot of undeveloped land and is also sitting on a vast portfolio of residential homes, waiting to be sold. In recent years, it has also actively expanded its build-to-rent platform and recently sold a big chunk of its rentals to unlock value for shareholders. Even then, it's today priced at a historically low valuation in large part due to the high interest rates making homeownership unaffordable:

2) D.R. Horton (DHI): Berkshire also initiated a new position in D.R. Horton, buying about a quarter billion worth of shares. It's yet another homebuilder with a similar business. It also owns a lot of land and homes, and it's now expanding its rental division. Just like Lennar, D.R. Horton is also discounted due to the surge in interest rates. It seems like a classic value play for Buffett.

3) Pool Corporation (POOL): Finally, Berkshire also bought a half billion worth of Pool, which is an indirect play on the recovery of the housing market in that it's the biggest pool distributor in the world. More home transactions result in more pool purchases.

In addition to that, Berkshire also initiated a position in a billboard REIT called Lamar Advertising (LAMR). This is their first REIT purchase in a while. Buffett has historically avoided investing in REITs, especially via Berkshire, explaining that it's not tax efficient for their structure and they get plenty of real estate exposure via their other investments anyway. Even then, Berkshire still bought about $150 million worth of shares in this initial purchase.

Lamar, like other REITs (VNQ), is today trading at a historically low valuation as its market sentiment has suffered in recent years due to the surge in interest rates.

All these recent real estate investments have one thing in common:

They are historically cheap due to the high interest rates.

The historic surge that began in 2022 made homeownership unaffordable and slowed transaction activity, hurting Lennar, D.R. Horton, and Pool. Meanwhile, it also hurt the market sentiment of REITs as investors pulled capital out of real estate to reallocate into fixed-income investments.

It seems that Buffett and/or the other leaders of Berkshire are seeing an opportunity in these historically low valuations, particularly as it appears increasingly likely that the Fed will soon cut interest rates, which could serve as a strong catalyst for all these stocks.

I don't think that it's a coincidence that they are now increasingly investing in these beaten-down real estate stocks right as the Fed is expected to start cutting interest rates.

According to FedWatch, the debt market is now pricing an 83% chance of rates being cut in just a few weeks from now, on Sept. 17:

The same tool is predicting an 84.4% chance of at least two or three rate cuts by the end of this year:

Finally, if we move forward one year, it's expected that interest rates will have been cut by 125 basis points by Sept. 16, 2026:

Why is the debt market predicting such steep rate cuts?

COVID-induced inflation has now been brought back under control. Real-time inflation has now been at right around 2% for the last year and a half:

It may increase a bit as a result of the tariffs, but as we explained in a recent analysis, this bump should be modest and temporary.

At the same time, the tariffs and the uncertainty that they cause also risk hurting the economy. We already had one surprisingly poor job report, likely at least in part as a result of it. When there's so much uncertainty, companies start delaying major investments and new hiring. At the same time, costs are going up for lots of companies, which may reduce profitability, further pushing them to delay new hiring decisions.

The combination of lower inflation and a weakening economy is likely to give the Fed what it needs to start cutting interest rates.

But even if the Fed Chair Jerome Powell refused to cut rates due to the uncertainty caused by the tariffs, keep in mind that his term will end in May 2026, and I think that it's very likely that President Donald Trump will then appoint someone who will give him the significant rate cuts that he has been asking for.

Therefore, Berkshire's bet on a recovery of real estate stocks makes a lot of sense. They're discounted and have a clear catalyst on the horizon.

But unlike Berkshire, which is buying mostly homebuilders and a little REITs, I prefer to allocate most of my capital towards REITs.

Their share prices have crashed even as their cash flows kept on rising, and as a result, they're today trading at near-decade low valuations, and this is almost entirely due to the surge in interest rates.

We think that the market overreacted, not understanding that REIT leverage was at an all-time low with LTVs of just 35% on average. Therefore, the impact of higher interest rates was not that significant. Meanwhile, REITs benefited from the higher inflation as it allowed them to grow their rents, which explains why their cash flows and dividends have kept growing in recent years.

I think that what happened is that when rates were ultra low, a lot of income-driven investors treated REITs as bond proxies. These investors never really cared about REITs, and so they sold them, irrespective of their fundamentals, as soon as they could earn a decent yield from Treasuries and money-market funds.

But I expect the opposite to now happen. As interest rates are cut, a lot of that same capital will flow right back into the REIT sector as yield-starved investors have few other options.

This will then push REITs to higher levels, resulting in upside for investors who position themselves today. Best of all, most of these REITs have relatively defensive business models, and you earn a high yield while you wait for the recovery.

That is why I prefer to position myself in REITs rather than in homebuilders to play the real estate recovery.

Higher yield, lower risk, similar upside potential.

What are the best REITs to buy today?

It's a tough question to answer, as most of them are heavily discounted.

Here are a few names worth considering:

NNN REIT (NNN) is a blue-chip net lease REIT with a 35-year track record of steady dividend growth, significant market outperformance, a BBB+ investment grade rating, and a defensive portfolio generating steady cash flow. Even then, it's today offered at a near 6% dividend yield, even as it retains 30% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth. When interest rates were lower, it commonly traded at closer to a 4.5% dividend yield, and returning there would result in about 30% upside.

Sila Realty Trust (SILA) is a healthcare REIT that today offers a 6.5% dividend yield that's well covered and backed by a very strong balance sheet. It has the least leverage in its peer group, with a very low 20% LTV, and we expect 5% annual growth in its cash flow and dividend going forward. As rates are cut, we expect investors to bid up and its yield to compress closer to 5%, which could result in 30% upside. We will patiently collect the rent checks while we wait.

Closing Note

It's interesting to see the "smart money" increasingly move to listed real estate companies as we now approach potential rate cuts in the near term.

Homebuilders are one way to play it.

REITs are another.

Both are fine and can make sense depending on your risk profile and need for income.

At High Yield Landlord, we mostly favor REITs as they are generally safer investments and offer high dividend income while we patiently wait for the upside.

data.title: Portfolio Review: 3 Stocks And 3 ETFs I'm Buying To Boost My Passive Income
data.description:
Just checked. The future is still uncertain.

In fact, it feels more uncertain than ever.

Some folks like Tom Lee think we are in the early stages of a new bull market. Others, like Moody's chief economist Mark Zandi and Apollo chief economist Torsten Slok, think we are on the precipice of something bad, whether that's recession or stagflation or both.

Depending who you believe, investors should either be doubling down on stocks or battening down the hatches and raising cash.

Me? Your humble scribe is too simple and conservative to veer from my tried-and-true strategy of dividend growth investing. But it would be fair to say that I'm tilting more toward defense than offense in my incremental capital allocation right now.

Here are the topics de la semaine:

  • How the negative effects of the frozen housing market might be on the verge of spilling out into the broader economy.
  • Some other signs of softness in the economy, but also an observation that growth stocks usually outperform value stocks.
  • A review of how my various dividend and income ETFs have performed over the last roughly year and a half, highlighting an interesting change in leadership.
  • Some comments on my 3-stock and 3-ETF buy list.

Onward!

Frozen Housing Market

The housing market has been largely frozen for quite some time now. So far, this has had a fairly minimal effect on the broader economy, but that may be about to change. The cumulative effects on near-record home buying unaffordability are mounting.

For example, although homebuilders have been able to maintain a steady pace of sales via mortgage rate write-downs, they appear to have at least temporarily overbuilt. The supply of new homes on the market has risen to nearly 10 months' worth.

I say "at least temporarily" because there is a strong demographic tailwind for the housing market, if houses were to become more affordable to the Millennial generation that are in their prime home buying years of life.

With homes remaining unaffordable, though, the growth in homeownership has ground to a halt. For the first time in a decade, the US homeowner population has stopped growing as would-be homebuyers stay trapped in the rental market.

It doesn't help that the economy more broadly appears to be stagnating, neither rebounding into faster growth nor slumping indomitably toward recession.

Between a sluggish labor market and zero net immigration, household formation (the growth in households, be they owner or renter) is dropping meaningfully.

Unaffordable homes, a soft jobs market, high economic uncertainty, and falling household formation are not the formula for a strong housing market. Just the opposite.

That makes the incredible rally in homebuilders (XHB) over the last several months somewhat puzzling to me. Notice in the chart below how homebuilder stock prices have totally diverged from residential construction spending, which is still trending down.

It appears to me that homebuilders are rallying mostly on Fed rate cut optimism and the market's belief that lower mortgage rates will come in their wake. But even if we do see somewhat lower mortgage rates, that will likely have only a minimal impact on new home starts and sales.

There are several reasons for this:

  1. The labor market is soft and appears to be getting softer. The unemployment rate is slowly trending higher, and hiring is anemic.
  2. There is already almost 10 months' worth of new home supply on the market.
  3. To whatever degree the cessation of net immigration affects household formation, there will simply be fewer potential homebuyers available to buy new homes.

As another tentative point, it's worth noting that seasonally adjusted residential construction payrolls are trending in the opposite direction as homebuilder stock prices. Employment in this industry appears to have peaked and begun a slow, gradual decline.

Now, residential construction employment would need to decline by quite a bit to actually signal recession. Notice that payrolls slid modestly in 2023 as a delayed effect from the rapid increase in mortgage rates in 2022, and yet it bottomed and rebounded that same year.

However, we are seeing one thing different about today's construction labor market than in 2023: worker quits are plunging.

This usually signifies a fragile labor market in which workers lack confidence in being able to secure another job in the same field.

Maybe, just maybe, the cracking of the housing market is the tip of the iceberg that will lead to broader economic weakness.

Miscellaneous Macro Menagerie

I mentioned economic stagnation above.

Obviously, in some areas like tech capex, the economy is booming. But in other areas, like the housing market and consumer spending, the stagnation becomes clear.

Notice, for example, that real (inflation-adjusted) retail sales have now been flat for the past four years.

The only reason nominal retail sales continue to grow is inflation. And higher retail prices are another reason why consumer sentiment is so low. Consumers' real purchasing power can't lift itself off the ground, which means consumers' real standards of living are not improving, at least not very fast.

The American people have been suffering a long hangover from the fiscal stimulus binge of 2020-2021. Free money felt good at the time, but the inflationary consequences of it have been painful and enduring.

And now we have artificial intelligence, a true double-edged sword that will likely boost worker productivity but is also taking its toll on white collar labor demand.

There has been much discussion of AI's negative impact on recent college graduates, but executives and managers haven't been spared either.

Since the beginning of 2022, white-collar employment across virtually all roles has declined.

Even if consumer spending isn't driving real (inflation-adjusted) growth in the US economy, AI is still a growth engine in itself.

One of the enviable aspects of the US economy is our tech sector, including the innovation incubator of Silicon Valley. The American culture of risk-taking for the sake of making great strides in innovation and technological advancement is an incredible strength in a world of slowing economic and population growth.

Perhaps it isn't surprising, then, to find that US growth stocks (VUG) have outperformed their value stock peers (VTV) the majority of the time over the last three decades:

Value has only shined for relatively brief stints after techy growth stocks pull back from overextension. After the Dot Com bubble of the late 1990s and the post-COVID speculative mania of 2021, value stocks enjoyed some time in the sun.

Otherwise, the rule seems to be that US growth stocks outperform.
